Just now

Press enter or click to view image in full size

CQRS is often explained in a way that makes it sound bigger than it needs to be.
You do not need event sourcing, multiple databases, or a distributed system to benefit from CQRS. In many ASP.NET Core applications, the practical value is simpler: commands change state, queries read state, and cross-cutting behavior is handled once in a pipeline.
That means validation, logging, transactions, and performance measurement do not need to be repeated in every endpoint.
In this article, we will build a production-style CQRS setup in ASP.NET Core using MediatR, FluentValidation, EF Core, transaction behaviors, query handlers, command handlers, and clean feature boundaries.
The Architecture
The API layer receives HTTP requests. Commands and queries represent application use cases. Handlers contain the use case logic. Pipeline behaviors wrap handlers with reusable cross-cutting policies.
This keeps endpoints thin and avoids the common problem where every service method manually repeats validation, logging, and transaction code.
Install Packages
These packages are enough for the core pattern.
